back_translation: In the church at the city of Antioch there were several prophets and teachers: Barnabas, Simeon who also had the name Niger, Lucius a Cyrenian, Manaen who had grown up together with Herod the tetrarch, and Saul.
While they were worshiping the Lord and fasting, the Holy Spirit spoke: 'Set apart Barnabas and Saul for Me for the work I have called the two of them to do.'
When they had fasted and prayed, they laid hands on Barnabas and Saul, and sent the two out.
As for Barnabas and Saul, when the Holy Spirit had sent them out, they went down to the city of Seleucia, and sailed from there to the island of Cyprus.
When they arrived at the city of Salamis, they proclaimed the word of God in the synagogues of the Jews. And John was with them as their helper also.
When they had traveled across that island as far as the city of Paphos, they found a certain Jewish man who was a magician and a false prophet, named Bar-Jesus.
He was with Sergius Paulus, the proconsul, who was a man of understanding. The proconsul summoned Barnabas and Saul, because he desired to hear the word of God.
But Elymas the magician (his name translates that way) opposed the two of them, trying to persuade the proconsul to turn from the faith.
As for Saul, who also had the name Paul, filled with the Holy Spirit, he stared fixedly at him,
and said, 'You who are full of every kind of deception and trickery, you are a child of the devil, enemy of every righteousness — will you never stop perverting the straight ways of the Lord?

Look — now the hand of the Lord will punish you. You will be blind, not seeing the sun for a while.' Immediately, a dark veil and darkness covered him, and he groped about seeking someone to lead him by the hand.
When the proconsul saw the event that had happened, he believed, because he was astonished at the teaching about the Lord.
When Paul and his traveling party had sailed out from the city of Paphos, they came to the city of Perga in the province of Pamphylia. As for John, he abandoned them and returned to Jerusalem.
But Paul and Barnabas continued their journey from Perga and arrived at the city of Pisidian Antioch. On the Sabbath day they entered the synagogue and sat down.
After the reading from the Law and the Prophets, the synagogue rulers sent word to them saying, 'Brethren, if you have any word that would encourage the people, please speak.'
Paul stood up, waved his hand, then said, 'You all — men of Israel and those who fear God — please listen to me.
The God of the people of Israel chose our fathers, and caused the people to multiply while they dwelled in the land of Egypt, and brought them out from there with His mighty arm,
then for about forty years He endured their conduct in the wilderness.
When He had destroyed seven nations in the land of Canaan, He granted the land of those nations as an inheritance to the people of Israel.
All of this took about four hundred fifty years. After that, He granted them judges until the time of Samuel the prophet.
Then they asked for a king, and God granted them Saul son of Kish, a man from the tribe of Benjamin, to be king for forty years.

When He had removed Saul from office, He raised up David to be their king, and He testified concerning David saying, 'I have found David son of Jesse, a man who pleases My heart; he will do according to My desires in every respect.'
From the lineage of this David, God brought Jesus, who is the Savior, to Israel according to the promise.
Before He would begin His ministry, John proclaimed beforehand a baptism of repentance to all the people of Israel.
As John was about to finish his ministry, he said, 'Whom do you all understand me to be? I am not that One. But look, He is coming after me, whose sandal-straps I am not worthy even to untie.'
Brothers all, descendants of Abraham, and you among us who fear God — this word of salvation has been sent to us.

For the people of Jerusalem and their rulers did not know Jesus and did not understand the words of the prophets that are read every Sabbath; yet when they judged Him, they in fact fulfilled those words.
Though they found no fault deserving death, they still asked Pilate to order the execution of Him.
When they had accomplished all that was written concerning Him, they took Him down from the tree and laid Him in a tomb.
But God caused Him to rise from death.
He appeared to those who had followed Him up from the region of Galilee to Jerusalem for many days, who now are witnesses on His side to the people.
And we two are bringing good news to proclaim to all of you — that is, the promise God granted to our ancestors.
God has caused this promise to be fulfilled for us all, who are the descendants of the ancestors, by raising Jesus up, as is written in the second psalm: 'You are My Son; today I have fathered you.'
As for the matter that God raised Him from death not to return to decay again, He spoke thus: 'I will grant to you all the holy and sure things promised to David.'
Because in another psalm, He spoke saying, 'You will not let Your Holy One undergo decay.'
David himself, when he had served the purpose of God in his own era, fell asleep and was buried with his ancestors, and underwent decay.
But the One whom God caused to rise did not undergo decay.

Therefore, brothers all, let you all know that through this Jesus the forgiveness of sins has been proclaimed to you, including from everything that the law of Moses was not able to make you righteous.
Everyone who believes in this One is righteous.
Therefore, take care, lest what the prophets spoke befall you all — namely,
'Look, you scoffers, marvel and perish, because I will do a work in the days of you all — a work that you all will not believe even if someone tells it to you.'
When Paul and Barnabas were about to leave the synagogue, the people begged them to come back and speak these words again on the next Sabbath.
When the synagogue meeting had ended, many of the Jews and devout proselytes followed Paul and Barnabas. The two spoke with them and urged them to remain in the grace of God.
When the following Sabbath arrived, nearly all the people of the city gathered to hear the word of the Lord.
When the Jewish leaders saw the crowd, they were filled with jealousy, and spoke against the words Paul was speaking, while blaspheming God.

Then Paul and Barnabas answered them boldly: 'It was necessary to proclaim the word of God to you all first. But since you push it aside and consider yourselves unworthy of eternal life, look — we are turning to the Gentiles now.
For the Lord has commanded us thus: "I have set you to be a light to the Gentiles, so that you will bring salvation to the ends of the earth."'
When the Gentiles heard this, they rejoiced and honored the word of the Lord, and everyone who had been appointed for eternal life came to believe.
The word of the Lord spread throughout that region.
But the Jewish leaders incited the devout women of high standing and the leaders of the city, and caused persecution of Paul and Barnabas, and drove the two out from the territory of their city.
Paul and Barnabas shook the dust from their feet as a protest against them, then went to the city of Iconium.
As for the disciples, they were filled with joy and with the Holy Spirit.
